TSX opens lower as tech jitters weigh
data.description:
Dec 11 (Reuters) - Canada's main stock index opened lower on Thursday, with technology stocks leading losses, as renewed fears about lofty valuations following Oracle's disappointing results outweighed a widely expected rate cut from the U.S. Federal Reserve.

At 9:31 a.m. ET, Toronto's S&P/TSX composite index was down 0.26% at 31,410.44 points.

(Reporting by Avinash P in Bengaluru; Editing by Sahal Muhammed)

SLB, Shell partner to develop AI-powered solutions for energy industry
data.description:
Dec 11 (Reuters) - SLB said on Thursday that it was partnering with Shell to develop digital and artificial intelligence tools aimed at improving performance and efficiency across upstream operations.

Shell and SLB will develop agentic AI-powered solutions designed to enhance the capabilities of technical experts and decision makers for Shell as well as the wider energy industry.

The deal extends a long-standing partnership between the two companies, after they partnered to deploy SLB's subsurface software, Petrel, across the London-listed company's assets worldwide.

(Reporting by Pranav Mathur in Bengaluru; Editing by Anil D'Silva)

US Jobless Claims Jump, Trade Deficit Unexpectedly Shrinks
data.description:
US Initial claims increased by 44,000 to 236,000 in the week ended Dec. 6, as applications for US unemployment benefits rose by the most since the onset of the pandemic. Meanwhile, the September US trade deficit unexpectedly narrowed to the smallest since mid-2020 as exports increased. Michael McKee reports on Bloomberg Television.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

S&P 500, Nasdaq poised for lower start on Oracle AI jitters
data.description:
Dec 11 (Reuters) - The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq are set to open lower on Thursday as fresh worries over Oracle's hefty AI spending plans overshadow optimism over a less hawkish tone from the Federal Reserve.

Oracle slumped 13% in premarket trading after its quarterly forecast fell short of analysts' estimates and the company said annual spending would climb $15 billion compared with its earlier expectation. 

The surge in costs, largely aimed at chasing AI cloud customers, raised concerns that Oracle is pouring money into artificial intelligence faster than it can turn those investments into profit.

Its shares are on track for their biggest quarterly loss since mid-2002 on worries that heavy reliance on debt financing could fuel an AI bubble similar to the dotcom bust of the early 2000s.

"Oracle has been the poster child for volatility and that will continue. The spending itself is stand-alone issue and part of the CapEx is a bit opaque," said Art Hogan, chief market strategist at B. Riley Wealth.

Shares of other artificial intelligence companies also fell in premarket trading.

Chipmakers Nvidia and Broadcom fell over 1% each, while hyperscalers such as Microsoft and Amazon.com slipped over 0.6% each and CoreWeave declined 4.8%. 

AI bubble concerns also hurt crypto stocks such as Strategy, which lost 2.7%, and Bit Digital, which fell 3% as bitcoin briefly slipped below $90,000.

"Any sort of pullback in the legacy mega-caps because of the reaction to Oracle would be a buying opportunity," Hogan added.

In a bright spot, Eli Lilly rose 1.2% after its next-generation obesity drug outperformed its blockbuster treatment Zepbound, further cementing the company's lead in the rapidly expanding weight-loss market.

At 8:36 a.m. ET, Dow E-minis were up 4 points, or 0.01%, S&P 500 E-minis were down 25.25 points, or 0.37%, Nasdaq 100 E-minis were down 143.25 points, or 0.56%.

Wall Street's fear gauge, the CBOE volatility index, rose 0.16 points to 15.93.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve lowered borrowing costs by 25 basis points as expected on Wednesday. Although Chair Jerome Powell signaled a pause on further easing, investors were relieved that rate hikes were not on the horizon, at a time when markets expect higher interest rates in other developed economies by the end of 2026. 

Traders see at least 50 bps of monetary easing next year on expectations that U.S. President Donald Trump's appointee to the fed chair will likely be a policy dove. White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett is the front-runner for the job. 

"We're not surprised to see near-term optimism in the markets given that the Fed continues to cut rates even though the economy is growing," said Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer at Northlight Asset Management.

"However, we think the rose-colored glasses may come off once investors realize that the path to lower interest rates may take longer - or may not materialize at all - to the extent that they believe it will."

Data from the Labor Department showed jobless claims rose 236,000 for the week ending December 6, compared to estimates of 220,000.

(Reporting by Johann M Cherian and Pranav Kashyap in Bengaluru; Editing by Sriraj Kalluvila and Tasim Zahid)

By Johann M Cherian and Pranav Kashyap

Ptc's Windchill+ Powering Holon's Development of One of the World's First Automotive-Standard Level-4 Evs
data.description:
PTC announced that HOLON is using its Windchill+ product lifecycle management (PLM) software-as-a-service (SaaS) solution to help develop and bring to market an autonomous Level-4 electric vehicle (EV) that complies with automotive industry requirements. HOLON is dedicated to reshaping public transportation with inclusive, autonomous, and sustainable mobility solutions. Its first vehicle, the HOLON urban, will offer fully barrier-free access and space for up to 15 passengers and is designed for scheduled routes, on-demand service, ride-pooling, and goods transport.

To support development, HOLON has introduced a flexible production concept that allows multiple vehicle variants to be built on the same assembly line, improving efficiency while advancing the company's sustainability goals. HOLON implemented Windchill+ in six months, creating a fully digital product development environment that replaces manual, paper-based processes with modern workflows. With real-time access to accurate product information through cloud-based PLM, teams can collaborate more efficiently across the development center and future manufacturing sites in Germany and the United States.

This digital foundation enables HOLON to move from design to production of the HOLON urban vehicle with the speed and agility needed to achieve its five-year vision of sustainable, inclusive mobility at scale. With Windchill+ and the rest of its portfolio, PTC is delivering on its vision for the Intelligent Product Lifecycle: enabling manufacturers and product companies to build a product data foundation in engineering and extend the value of that data across their enterprise. With a product data foundation, companies like HOLON can also accelerate AI-driven transformation across their organization.

Broader use of product data enables companies to bring higher quality products to market faster, better manage product complexity, meet regulatory and compliance standards, and much more.

Canada Swings to Modest Goods-Trade Surplus in September -- Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


OTTAWA--A jump in goods exports swung Canada to its first trade surplus in eight months in September, as shipments rebounded even as domestic demand showed signs of softening.

The country recorded a modest merchandise-trade surplus of 153 million Canadian dollars, the equivalent of about $110.9 million, Statistics Canada said Thursday.

That was far from the C$4.5 billion deficit economists anticipated, and came after Canada ran a shortfall of C$6.43 billion the month before.

Canada's longstanding surplus with the U.S. widened to the most since February as exports rebounded.

The value of Canadian goods exports jumped 6.3% in September to C$64.23 billion, the strongest advance in seven months. That marked a solid recovery after shipments the month before fell 3.2%, driven by a drop in exports to the U.S. as tariffs and trade tensions weighed.

Imports for September dropped 4.1% from the month before to C$64.08 billion, more than countering a 1.0% rise in August.

Overall, exports were higher in nine of the 11 product categories tracked by Statistics Canada, led by shipments of metals and mineral products thanks to a surge in unwrought gold. Excluding precious metals and their alloys, total exports were up 4.5% for the month.

Exports of energy products also were up strongly, with a fifth straight month of increased crude oil exports driving the gain with a rise in volumes.

Imports meanwhile were dragged lower by a drop in incoming shipments of metals and minerals, mainly unwrought gold. Stripping out precious metals, imports were down 1.5%.

Canada's export to the U.S., by far its largest market, rose 4.6% to C$45.84 billion thanks in part to shipments across the border of aircraft, light trucks and gold. Imports from Canada's nearest neighbor were down 1.7% fall to C$37.22 billion.

Exports to countries other than the U.S. rose a strong 11.0% for the latest month, while imports from these countries fell 7.3%. That narrowed Canada's goods-trade deficit with countries beyond the U.S. to roughly C$8.5 billion, the lowest since last October, from an all-time high of C$12.4 billion in August.

The delayed trade data comes a day after the Bank of Canada held its policy interest rate steady, the start of what economists expect will be a long pause after rates were rolled back over a roughly 16-month period to subdue inflation. The central bank continues to project weak economic growth in the final quarter of the year and a soft 1.1% expansion in 2026, despite a stronger-than-expected rebound in activity in the second quarter.

Gross domestic product expanded 2.6% annualized in the third quarter thanks in large part to the trade balance strengthening, with Statistics Canada estimating exports of goods and services edged up 0.2% but imports dropped 2.2%. Those trade figures are subject to revision because of the delay in receiving data from the U.S. during the shutdown.

When international trade in goods and international trade in services were combined, Canadian exports rose 5.0%, while imports declined 3.0%, Statistics Canada said Thursday. As a result, Canada swung to a trade surplus incorporating both goods and services of C$327 million in September from a C$6.25 billion deficit a month earlier.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

12-11-25 0914ET
